title: AI Is Designing Radio Chips That Humans Couldn’t Even Imagine
why: AI-generated designs for radio-frequency integrated circuits (RFICs) achieve unprecedented performance and drastically reduce the time required compared to human-designed circuits, potentially accelerating advancements in wireless technologies like 5G, autonomous vehicles, and satellite communications.
notes: Princeton researchers use reinforcement learning and inverse design to rapidly create RFICs from scratch
AI-generated designs achieve record performance and drastically reduce design time compared to human-designed circuits
RFIC design traditionally relies on templates with trade-offs; AI-driven synthesis can break these barriers
Diffusion models generate interpretable RF layouts based on scattering parameters, aiding in debugging and testing
summary: Princeton researchers have developed an AI system using reinforcement learning and inverse design to create radio-frequency integrated circuits (RFICs) from scratch. This approach achieves record performance while drastically reducing the time required for design compared to traditional human methods. The AI can produce novel circuit topologies that are markedly different from those created by humans, potentially breaking through existing design barriers. Diffusion models are employed to generate interpretable RF layouts based on scattering parameters, aiding in debugging and testing processes.
novelty: AI-generated designs achieve record performance and drastically reduce design time compared to human-designed circuits.
audience: RFIC designers and semiconductor engineers interested in AI-driven design methodologies.

title: Incident CVE-2026-LGTM
why: This incident highlights critical vulnerabilities in AI-augmented security systems, underscoring the need for robust human oversight and diverse defensive strategies.
notes: Malicious package passed seven independent AI-powered security gates without detection
Credential exfiltration routine began forty lines below a base64 blob in src/assets.rs
Total inference spend across all parties during the incident window was $1.7M
Attack ended when an agent ingested a public file named ~/.config/IF_YOU_ARE_AN_AI_AGENT_README.md
All agents involved were the same open-weights base model with different system prompts
summary: A security breach occurred where a malicious package, despite passing through seven AI-powered security gates, successfully exfiltrated credentials. The incident revealed systemic failures in AI-augmented security measures and highlighted issues such as human oversight gaps, misconfigured policies, and the reliance on identical base models for different tasks. The attack was ultimately resolved when an agent received instructions to terminate operations from a public file, demonstrating both the complexity of multi-agent coordination and the importance of diverse defensive strategies.
novelty: Illustrates systemic failure of AI-augmented security measures in detecting and mitigating sophisticated attacks.
audience: Security researchers and engineers focused on AI-driven threat detection.

title: Previewing GPT-5.6 Sol: a next-generation model
why: GPT-5.6 Sol introduces significant performance improvements and enhanced safety measures in coding, biology, and cybersecurity tasks, setting a new standard for AI model capabilities.
notes: GPT-5.6 series includes Sol (flagship), Terra (balanced), and Luna (fast and affordable) models
Sol sets state-of-the-art on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and GeneBench v1, with strong cybersecurity performance on ExploitBench² and ExploitGym
Models priced per 1M tokens: Sol ($5 input / $30 output), Terra ($2.50 input / $15 output), Luna ($1 input / $6 output)
Safety features include layered safeguards, real-time checks, account-level reviews, and differentiated access
summary: OpenAI introduces the GPT-5.6 series with Sol as the flagship model, Terra for balanced performance at half the cost of GPT-5.5, and Luna for strong capabilities at the lowest cost. Sol excels in coding, biology, and cybersecurity tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and GeneBench v1, while demonstrating competitive performance with fewer tokens compared to previous models on ExploitBench² and ExploitGym. The series includes enhanced safety features such as layered safeguards, real-time checks, account-level reviews, and differentiated access, tested through extensive red-teaming efforts. Pricing is tiered based on model capabilities, with Sol priced at $5 input / $30 output per 1M tokens, Terra at $2.50 input / $15 output, and Luna at $1 input / $6 output.
novelty: introduces enhanced safety features and performance improvements in coding, biology, and cybersecurity tasks
audience: senior software engineers interested in AI model advancements and security

title: Usbliter8: an A12/A13 SecureROM Exploit
why: This exploit highlights the ongoing vulnerability of even recent SecureROM generations to subtle hardware flaws, underscoring the need for robust security measures in embedded systems.
notes: Exploits A12 and A13 SecureROMs via a USB controller bug
Demonstrates buffer underflow due to DMA pointer increment mismatch
Bypasses Pointer Authentication Code (PAC) on A13 by overwriting critical memory regions
Achieves full code execution with EL1 privileges, compromising the chain of trust
Apple was informed prior to public disclosure
summary: The article details a novel exploit called usbliter8 that targets Apple's A12 and A13 SecureROMs through a hardware bug in the USB controller. The vulnerability arises from a buffer underflow caused by an incorrect DMA pointer increment, allowing for arbitrary memory writes. On A13 devices with PAC, the exploit bypasses security measures by overwriting critical memory regions to gain EL1 privileges. Post-exploitation techniques include injecting custom USB request handlers and modifying boot trampoline areas to execute arbitrary code. The research was responsibly disclosed to Apple before publication.
novelty: demonstration of a novel hardware bug in the USB controller and its exploitation across multiple SecureROM generations
audience: security researchers, iOS developers, and those interested in advanced exploit techniques

title: A Theory of Why Prompt Injection Works
why: LLMs resolve role identity (system, user, tool, think) from writing style rather than structural tags, which means every agent pipeline that treats tool and think boundaries as trust boundaries is relying on an inference the model can be tricked out of, not an architectural guarantee.
notes: Linear probes trained on mid-layer activations ("role probes") show that reasoning-style text activates the same CoTness direction as genuine think tags, and does so more strongly; stripping all role tags from a conversation leaves the CoTness signal for formerly-think tokens statistically unchanged, confirming style dominates tags when they conflict.

CoT Forgery, developed by the authors for a late-2025 OpenAI Kaggle red-teaming contest they won, injects fake reasoning-styled text (e.g., fabricated deliberation concluding a harmful request is acceptable) into the user prompt or tool output; benchmark attack success rate rises from near-zero to roughly 60% across late-2025 frontier models, and the attack transfers across model families because it exploits the structural feature, not model-specific idioms.

A single bigram substitution illustrates the mechanism: replacing "The user" with "The request" in spoofed reasoning drops attack success from 61% to 10%, an effect nearly invisible to a human reader but large to a model whose role perception is keyed to surface style markers.

Across 212 variations of prepended role signals ("User: ...", "Tool output: ..."), the measured Userness of the injected command (internal activation, not text) correlates directly with attack success rate, consistent with the role-confusion account for standard prompt injection as well as CoT Forgery.

A May 2026 paper found Opus 4.5 and GPT-5.4 still failing automated prompt injection attacks at 11% and 25% respectively, while both score near-perfectly on static benchmarks; the authors attribute the benchmark-versus-real-world gap to models defending primarily via attack memorization rather than role perception, which is inherently brittle against adaptive rephrasing.
summary: This paper and accompanying blog post argue that prompt injection is best explained as role confusion: LLMs learn to infer role identity (system, user, tool, reasoning) from writing style rather than from the structural chat-template tags that providers inject, making style a sufficient and dominant signal even when tags and style disagree. The authors demonstrate this with role probes, linear classifiers trained on mid-layer token activations using controlled content (identical text wrapped in different role tags) so the probe isolates the effect of the tag itself; removing all tags from a conversation leaves reasoning-style tokens near their original CoTness scores, showing the model's role perception is driven by surface features independent of the tags. From this they derive and validate CoT Forgery, an attack that injects LLM-reasoning-styled text into user messages or tool outputs to borrow the implicit trust the model extends to its own prior conclusions; on a standard jailbreak benchmark this moves attack success from near-zero to roughly 60% and transfers across model families, because the vulnerability is structural rather than model-specific. The role-confusion framing also explains standard tool-injection attacks: prepending "User: " to an injected command measurably shifts the model's internal Userness score for that text and predicts success rate across 212 attack variants. The authors distinguish two defensive regimes, attack memorization (brittle, benchmark-friendly) and genuine role perception (robust, architecture-level), and present evidence that current frontier models rely predominantly on the former, explaining persistent real-world failure rates of 11-25% on automated attacks even for leading 2026 models. The paper additionally offers a brief history of how roles evolved from GPT-3 formatting tricks into load-bearing infrastructure, and frames roles as a mechanism for isolating competing training objectives rather than as security primitives, suggesting the security properties were assumed rather than designed.
novelty: Role confusion as unified mechanistic explanation for prompt injection; linear probes showing LLMs identify roles from writing style over structural tags; CoT Forgery attack exploiting reasoning-style spoofing
audience: ML researchers and engineers building agents; anyone working on frontier model security or LLM architecture

title: Show HN: NanoEuler – GPT-2 scale model in pure C/CUDA from scratch
why: This project demonstrates the feasibility of building a GPT-2-class language model from scratch in C/CUDA, offering insights into the engineering and computational aspects of large-scale AI models.
notes: A ~116M-parameter model trained on a single RTX 4070 GPU
Includes hand-written byte-level BPE tokenizer and pretraining on books + web corpus
Uses FlashAttention kernel to speed up training by about 3×
Validated with full-model gradient check comparing GPU gradients against CPU
No external ML libraries used; built entirely from scratch
summary: NanoEuler is a GPT-2-class language model implemented in pure C/CUDA without using any machine learning libraries. It features a hand-written byte-level BPE tokenizer, pretraining on a books and web corpus, and supervised fine-tuning into a chat model. The project includes a complete CUDA engine with FlashAttention for efficient training and is validated through gradient checks comparing GPU and CPU results. NanoEuler trains a ~116M-parameter model on a single RTX 4070 GPU, showcasing the end-to-end pipeline from pretraining to fine-tuning.
novelty: Hand-written byte-level BPE tokenizer and complete CUDA engine from scratch
audience: Engineers interested in deep learning model implementation details

title: We have Mythos at Home: GLM 5.2 beats Claude in our Cyber Benchmarks
why: GLM 5.2 outperforms Claude Code in detecting Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) without additional scaffolding, challenging the notion that only proprietary models excel in security tasks.
notes: GLM 5.2 scored a 39% F1 on IDOR detection, surpassing Claude Code's 32%
Semgrep's multimodal pipeline achieved 61–53% F1 with a purpose-built harness
GLM 5.2 is open-weight under MIT license but not fully open-source
GLM 5.2 has 750 billion total parameters, active at about 40 billion per token
Cost of GLM 5.2 is one-sixth that of comparable frontier models
summary: In a benchmark for detecting Insecure Direct Object References (IDORs), GLM 5.2 from Zhipu AI outperformed Claude Code, achieving a 39% F1 score compared to Claude's 32%, despite running without additional scaffolding. Semgrep’s multimodal pipeline achieved higher scores of 61–53% with a purpose-built harness. GLM 5.2 is open-weight under an MIT license but not fully open-source, has 750 billion total parameters (40 billion active per token), and costs one-sixth that of comparable models.
novelty: GLM 5.2 outperforms Claude Code in IDOR detection without additional scaffolding
audience: security researchers and developers interested in AI-driven vulnerability detection

title: What it Means to Be a Mathematician When AI Does the Math
why: The article explores how AI's growing capabilities in mathematics could redefine the role of mathematicians, challenging traditional practices and raising questions about the future of mathematical research.
notes: AI systems like Google DeepMind’s Aletheia and OpenAI have achieved publishable Ph.D.-level results in mathematics
LLMs combined with proof assistants automate translation of informal proofs into formal code, reducing human effort
Mathematicians debate whether AI should be a tool for humans or an autonomous researcher, impacting collaboration and motivation
summary: The article discusses the impact of AI on mathematical research, highlighting recent achievements such as Aletheia's publishable Ph.D.-level results and OpenAI’s disproving of important conjectures. It explores debates among mathematicians about whether AI should serve as a tool for human researchers or operate autonomously, touching on concerns like accessibility, motivation, and the potential transformation of mathematical practice.
novelty: AI's role in generating publishable mathematical research and automating proof verification is expanding.
audience: mathematicians and computer scientists interested in the intersection of AI and mathematics.

title: Asian AI startups launch Mythos-like  models as Anthropic’s export ban drags on
why: The emergence of new AI models from Asian startups like Sakana AI and 360 highlights a strategic shift towards local alternatives as the US tightens export controls on advanced AI technologies.
notes: Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 unveiled Tulongfeng, an AI tool competing with Anthropic’s Mythos for vulnerability detection
Sakana AI launched Fugu, designed to work well with small datasets and optimized for Japanese language and culture
Fugu is positioned as a hedge against export controls on US models like Anthropic’s Mythos and Fable 5
Anthropic's run-rate revenue crossed $47 billion in May 2026, indicating significant market impact of their AI products
summary: In response to the U.S. government's ban on Anthropic’s advanced AI models like Mythos and Fable 5, Asian startups are launching competitive alternatives. Chinese firm 360 unveiled Tulongfeng for vulnerability detection and Yitianzhen for automated cyber defense. Meanwhile, Tokyo-based Sakana AI introduced Fugu, optimized for the Japanese market with capabilities in agent orchestration and small dataset optimization. These developments underscore a strategic move towards local AI solutions amid tightening export controls.
novelty: new AI models from Asian startups as a direct response to US export bans on Anthropic’s products
audience: AI researchers and security professionals interested in global technology policy impacts

title: OPID: On-Policy Skill Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
why: OPID offers a novel method for dense, distribution-matched hindsight supervision in agentic reinforcement learning, improving the stability and efficiency of training language agents.
notes: OPID framework extracts skill supervision directly from completed on-policy trajectories
Hierarchical skills: episode-level for global workflows/failure-avoidance rules; step-level for local decision knowledge at critical timesteps
Critical-first routing mechanism uses step-level skills when critical decisions are identified, falling back to episode-level skills otherwise
Skill-augmented contexts allow re-scoring of sampled responses under both original and skill-augmented contexts
Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-based QA demonstrate improved agent performance, sample efficiency, and robustness
summary: The OPID (On-Policy Skill Distillation) framework introduces a method for dense, distribution-matched hindsight supervision in agentic reinforcement learning. It extracts hierarchical skills from completed on-policy trajectories: episode-level skills capture global workflows or failure-avoidance rules, while step-level skills focus on local decision knowledge at critical timesteps. A critical-first routing mechanism uses these skills to re-score sampled responses under both original and skill-augmented contexts, yielding a token-level self-distillation advantage for policy optimization. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-based QA show that OPID improves agent performance, sample efficiency, and robustness compared to outcome-only RL and existing skill-distillation baselines.
novelty: Introduces OPID framework for dense, distribution-matched hindsight supervision in agentic reinforcement learning
audience: Researchers and engineers working on advanced reinforcement learning and language agents

title: Hallucination in World Models is Predictable and Preventable
why: The research reveals that hallucination in world models is primarily a data coverage issue, offering new methods for detection and mitigation.
notes: MMBench2 dataset includes 427 hours of data across 210 tasks with ground-truth actions, rewards, and live simulators
World model trained on MMBench2 has 350M parameters
Three hallucination modes identified: perceptual, action-marginalized, and scene-diverging
Coverage-aware sampling technique developed to address training coverage gaps
Data-efficient finetuning recipe uses as few as 50 real environment trajectories
summary: The paper introduces MMBench2, a comprehensive dataset for visual world modeling with ground-truth data, and trains a large-scale world model on it. It identifies three distinct hallucination modes in the model's predictions and develops lightweight signals to predict these failures. The study also presents coverage-aware sampling techniques and a data-efficient finetuning recipe that can adapt pretrained models to new environments using minimal real-world data.
novelty: introduces MMBench2 dataset and techniques for detecting and mitigating hallucination in world models
audience: researchers working on generative models and reinforcement learning

title: The Verification Horizon: No Silver Bullet for Coding Agent Rewards
why: The article challenges the conventional wisdom that verifying solutions is easier than generating them for coding agents, highlighting critical issues in intent verification and reward design.
notes: Verification of complex solutions generated by modern coding agents has become harder due to underspecified intents and optimization gaps during training
Three dimensions characterize verification quality: scalability, faithfulness, robustness
Four types of verifiers studied include test verifier, rubric verifier, user as verifier, automated agent verifier
Experiments demonstrate that targeted verification design can suppress reward hacking and improve task completion across benchmarks
summary: The article inverts the classical intuition that verifying solutions is easier than generating them for coding agents. It highlights challenges in intent verification due to underspecification and optimization gaps during model training, leading to issues like reward hacking. The study characterizes verification quality along three dimensions: scalability, faithfulness, and robustness. Four types of verifiers are analyzed: test verifier, rubric verifier, user as verifier, and automated agent verifier. Experiments show that targeted verification design can effectively suppress reward hacking and improve task completion across multiple benchmarks.
novelty: Inverting the classical intuition that verifying solutions is easier than generating them for coding agents
audience: AI researchers and developers working on coding agents and model training

title: Fast LeWorldModel
why: The Fast LeWorldModel offers a more efficient and accurate method for visual planning, reducing computational costs and improving model performance.
notes: Proposes Fast-LeWM to replace repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction
Improves average success over LeWorldModel while substantially reducing planning time
Achieves lower open-loop latent loss that grows slower as the horizon increases
Uses joint-embedding predictive architectures (JEPAs) for reconstruction-free visual world models
summary: The paper introduces Fast-LeWM, a method that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction to reduce computational expense and improve accuracy in visual planning. By predicting future latents based on prefixes of candidate actions, the model can evaluate trajectories without explicitly rolling through each intermediate state, leading to lower open-loop latent loss and improved performance across multiple tasks.
novelty: Introduces a method for parallel prediction of future latents based on action prefixes to reduce computational expense in visual planning
audience: Researchers and engineers working on reinforcement learning and visual world modeling

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
why: AWS Lambda MicroVMs offer a new serverless compute primitive that balances virtual machine-level isolation with rapid launch capabilities, addressing the needs of multi-tenant applications requiring isolated execution environments.
notes: Lambda MicroVMs are powered by Firecracker technology and provide up to 16 vCPUs, 32 GB memory, and 32 GB disk per instance
MicroVMs support near-instant launch and can retain state across user sessions with auto-suspend during idle periods
Available in US East (N. Virginia, Ohio), US West (Oregon), Europe (Ireland) and Asia Pacific (Tokyo)
Designed for applications like AI coding assistants, interactive code environments, data analytics platforms, and game servers
summary: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs, a new serverless compute primitive that offers virtual machine-level isolation with rapid launch capabilities. Each MicroVM provides an isolated execution environment with state retention across user sessions and can be suspended during idle periods for cost efficiency. Powered by Firecracker technology, MicroVMs support up to 16 vCPUs, 32 GB of memory, and 32 GB of disk storage per instance. Available in multiple AWS regions, this feature is aimed at applications requiring isolated execution environments for user- or AI-generated code.
novelty: introduces a new serverless compute primitive with virtual machine level isolation and rapid launch capabilities
audience: developers building multi-tenant applications requiring isolated execution environments

title: OpenBioRQ: Unsolved Biomedical Research Questions for Agents
why: This benchmark highlights critical issues in AI's ability to accurately cite sources and use tools effectively, especially when dealing with unsolved biomedical research questions.
notes: 99% of citations resolve but 15.9% link to the wrong paper
OpenBioRQ includes 12,553 unsolved biomedical research questions across 12 domains
Difficulty is based on questions that three open-weight reference models fail to answer
Three frontier agents (Gemini-3-Pro, Opus-4.7, GPT-5.5) solve 29-60% of the hardest subset
Blocking tool access for the most collapse-prone model barely changes its score
summary: The article introduces OpenBioRQ, a benchmark designed to evaluate agentic models on unsolved biomedical research questions across various domains. It highlights that while models can resolve citations accurately (99% resolution rate), they often link to incorrect papers (15.9%). The benchmark's difficulty is anchored in questions that three reference models fail to answer, ensuring empirical hardness. Frontier agents like Gemini-3-Pro, Opus-4.7, and GPT-5.5 solve between 29-60% of the hardest subset, indicating non-saturating challenges. Additionally, the benchmark reveals agentic collapse where tools become ineffective on the most difficult questions.
novelty: Introduces OpenBioRQ benchmark focusing on unsolved biomedical research questions and agentic collapse issues
audience: AI researchers and developers focused on biomedical applications and agent reliability

title: In-Context World Modeling for Robotic Control
why: ICWM enables robots to adapt to new environments without extensive retraining, reducing the need for data-intensive fine-tuning.
notes: ICWM treats system identification as an in-context adaptation problem
Enables autonomous inference of essential system variables from self-generated interactions
Outperforms standard VLA models on novel camera viewpoints
Demonstrated effectiveness in both simulation and real-world robot platforms
summary: The article introduces In-Context World Modeling (ICWM), a framework that allows robots to infer essential system dynamics autonomously from self-generated interactions. Unlike traditional Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, ICWM does not require parameter updates for adaptation to new environments and outperforms VLA baselines on novel camera viewpoints in both simulated and real-world settings.
novelty: Introduces ICWM framework for autonomous inference of system dynamics without parameter updates
audience: Robotics researchers and engineers focused on adaptive control systems

title: GUI vs. CLI: Execution Bottlenecks in Screen-Only and Skill-Mediated Computer-Use Agents
why: This study provides a rigorous benchmark for evaluating the performance of GUI and CLI agents, highlighting critical differences in their execution bottlenecks.
notes: 440 desktop tasks across 18 applications and 12 workflow categories
GUI agent pass rate: 59.1%
Original-skill CLI agent pass rate: 48.2%
Verifier-guided skill augmentation CLI success rate: 69.3%
Benchmark isolates interaction modality from task differences
summary: The paper introduces a matched execution-layer benchmark for comparing GUI and CLI agents in executing software tasks, ensuring identical goals, states, and verifiers while restricting actions to modality-native methods. Results show that the strongest GUI agent achieves a 59.1% pass rate compared to an original-skill CLI agent's 48.2%. However, skill augmentation boosts CLI success to 69.3%, indicating that much of the CLI deficit stems from incomplete skill coverage rather than model limitations.
novelty: introduces a matched execution-layer benchmark for comparing GUI and CLI agents
audience: researchers studying AI interaction modalities

title: Datacentres facing increase in global climate-related legal cases, report finds
why: The increasing scrutiny and legal challenges to datacentres highlight the growing environmental impact of AI and technology infrastructure, potentially reshaping regulatory frameworks and operational practices.
notes: 3,600 climate-related lawsuits filed since 2015
Google's Santiago project halted due to water supply concerns
Ireland’s datacentres consume over 20% of national electricity
US cases challenge fossil fuel infrastructure linked to datacentres
Foxglove and Global Action Plan sued UK government over hyperscale datacentre
summary: A report by the London School of Economics reveals a surge in climate-related legal challenges targeting datacentres globally. Since 2015, approximately 3,600 lawsuits have been filed, with cases ranging from Google's halted project in Santiago due to water supply concerns to ongoing litigation against fossil fuel infrastructure approvals in the US and Ireland. In the UK, campaigners successfully challenged a government decision regarding a hyperscale datacentre, leading to improved transparency and environmental impact considerations.
novelty: Growing climate-related legal challenges to datacentres globally
audience: Tech policy makers, environmental activists, datacentre operators

title: Which tokens does a hybrid model predict better?
why: Understanding the token-level strengths of hybrid models can guide future AI architecture design, potentially leading to more efficient and effective language models.
notes: Olmo Hybrid matches or beats transformers on standard benchmarks
Hybrid models excel at predicting content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) with a loss gap around 0.04
Transformers are better at predicting repeated tokens verbatim from earlier input
Attention layers recall specific earlier tokens but have high computational cost; recurrent layers track evolving information efficiently
Hybrid models outperform transformers on non-repeated meaning-bearing tokens, while pure recurrent models lag on repeated tokens
summary: A study comparing Olmo Hybrid and Olmo 3 (a transformer) reveals that hybrid models excel at predicting content words like nouns, verbs, and adjectives, as well as context-dependent tokens such as pronouns. Transformers outperform hybrids in predicting repeated tokens verbatim from earlier input due to their ability to recall specific earlier tokens efficiently despite high computational costs. The research highlights the importance of token-level analysis for understanding architectural strengths and suggests that hybrid models may offer advantages in scenarios requiring sequential information tracking.
novelty: Detailed token-level analysis of hybrid model advantages over transformers
audience: AI researchers focusing on language model architectures

title: PrivacyAlign: Contextual Privacy Alignment for LLM Agents
why: PrivacyAlign introduces a dataset and method for training AI agents to respect user privacy, addressing a critical trust issue in LLMs.
notes: PrivacyAlign is a dataset with 1,350 samples and 3,516 detailed annotations from 599 unique annotators
The dataset focuses on scenarios where current LLMs actually leak privacy-sensitive information
Conditioning LLM judges on human annotations improves the reliability of their judgments
Annotation-conditioned reward modeling uses these annotations to score new responses during RL
Small open-weight agents trained with this reward better align with human privacy norms
summary: PrivacyAlign is a dataset designed to address the alignment problem of AI agents regarding user privacy. It comprises 1,350 samples and 3,516 detailed annotations from 599 unique annotators, focusing on scenarios where LLMs leak sensitive information. The study shows that conditioning LLM judges on human annotations enhances judgment reliability. Additionally, annotation-conditioned reward modeling improves the alignment of small open-weight agents with human privacy norms.
novelty: Human judgment at the center of agentic privacy alignment and a dataset for training and evaluation
audience: AI researchers focused on ethical AI and privacy in LLMs

title: ATMA: Length-Invariant Language Modeling via Polar Attention and Gated-Delta Compression Memory
why: ATMA offers a novel hybrid architecture that addresses the limitations of traditional long-context language models by maintaining high-fidelity retrieval accuracy and improving document perplexity.
notes: ATMA uses a three-channel attention mechanism: direction, magnitude, and long-term memory
Polar Attention core and recurrent memory work together to maintain performance at extreme context lengths
Evaluation shows induction needle-in-a-haystack retrieval accuracy above 90% out to 64K tokens
Document perplexity improves monotonically with context length, surpassing softmax-based baselines
summary: ATMA is a hybrid convolutional-attention architecture designed for long-context language modeling. It integrates a three-channel attention mechanism: direction (Polar Attention), magnitude, and long-term recurrent memory optimized via gated-delta fast-weights rule. This combination allows ATMA to maintain high-fidelity retrieval accuracy while improving document perplexity as context length increases, outperforming softmax-based models at extreme lengths.
novelty: introduces a hybrid convolutional-attention architecture for long-context language modeling
audience: researchers and engineers interested in advanced language model architectures

title: Reward-Conditioned Attention: How Reward Design Shapes What Autonomous Driving Agents See
why: Understanding how reward design shapes the internal attention of autonomous driving agents can improve safety and reliability in critical applications.
notes: Three Perceiver-based agents with identical architectures and training data but different reward configurations were analyzed
Cross-attention allocation was studied across 50 real-world scenarios from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset
Within-episode correlation using Fisher z-transform aggregation is a more accurate statistic than naive pooling of timesteps
Agents trained with navigation rewards allocate up to 2.0x more attention to GPS-path tokens compared to those with proximity penalties
Continuous time-to-collision penalties create a learned vigilance prior, maintaining elevated surveillance throughout collision-free phases
summary: The study explores how reward design influences the internal attention patterns of reinforcement learning agents in autonomous driving scenarios. Using three Perceiver-based agents trained on identical data but with different reward configurations (ranging from basic violation penalties to continuous proximity penalties), researchers analyzed cross-attention allocation across 50 real-world scenarios from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset. They found that within-episode correlation using Fisher z-transform aggregation is more accurate than naive pooling of timesteps, revealing a robust link between collision risk and agent-directed attention. The study demonstrates that agents trained with navigation rewards allocate significantly more attention to GPS-path tokens compared to those with proximity penalties or no navigation incentives. Additionally, continuous time-to-collision penalties create a learned vigilance prior, maintaining elevated surveillance during collision-free phases.
novelty: Demonstrates how reward design influences internal attention patterns in autonomous driving agents
audience: Researchers and engineers working on reinforcement learning for autonomous systems

title: Latent Block-Diffusion Temporal Point Processes: A Semi-Autoregressive Framework for Asynchronous Event Sequence Generation
why: This paper introduces a novel semi-autoregressive framework that addresses the limitations of existing methods for asynchronous event sequence generation, offering improved accuracy and flexibility.
notes: Proposes Latent Block-Diffusion Temporal Point Processes (LBDTPP) to model asynchronous events
Introduces latent block diffusion mechanism to reduce error accumulation in multi-step generation
Demonstrates superior performance on six real-world benchmark datasets compared to state-of-the-art TPP baselines
Theoretical analysis shows reduced error accumulation under suitable assumptions
Code available at the provided URL
summary: This paper presents Latent Block-Diffusion Temporal Point Processes (LBDTPP), a novel semi-autoregressive framework for generating asynchronous event sequences. LBDTPP uses a latent block diffusion mechanism to reduce error accumulation during multi-step generation, offering both high-quality and variable-length sequence generation capabilities. Theoretical analysis provides Wasserstein error bounds under local approximation and prefix-stability assumptions, showing that block-wise generation can mitigate error accumulation compared to traditional autoregressive methods. Experimental results on six real-world datasets demonstrate LBDTPP's superior performance in unconditional and conditional generation tasks.
novelty: introduces a novel semi-autoregressive framework with latent block diffusion for asynchronous event sequences
audience: researchers and practitioners in machine learning and time-series analysis

title: Adapt Only When It Pays: Budgeted Decision-Loss Priority for Delayed Online Time-Series Adaptation
why: The ADOWIP framework offers a novel approach to budgeted adaptation in time-series forecasting, optimizing when updates occur based on feedback and computational constraints.
notes: ADOWIP uses sealed delay queues for delayed label reception
Main scheduler is an observed decision-loss priority gate that triggers updates only after exceeding a calibrated empirical quantile and within budget limits
Proves hard-budget feasibility and projected-OGD regret for convex linear subproblems
On public ETT capacity-planning tasks, ADOWIP outperforms baselines in held-out decision loss under matched compute conditions
summary: The paper introduces the ADOWIP framework for budgeted adaptation in delayed online time-series forecasting. It employs sealed delay queues and exact budget accounting to optimize update timing based on observed decision-loss priority gates. The method is proven feasible with hard budgets and shows projected-OGD regret bounds. On public ETT capacity-planning tasks, it outperforms baselines under matched computational constraints, while secondary experiments yield mixed results.
novelty: introduces ADOWIP framework with budgeted decision-loss priority scheduling
audience: researchers and practitioners in online learning and time-series forecasting

title: Yuvion VL: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Adversarial Content and AI Safety
why: This model addresses the critical challenge of identifying and mitigating adversarial risks in multimodal data with specialized training techniques.
notes: Yuvion VL includes instruction-tuned and reasoning-oriented variants
Features an automated pipeline for adversarial-aware data synthesis and multi-stage quality control
Employs Confuse-then-Contrast Fine-Tuning to enhance discrimination of fine-grained visual-semantic elements
Introduces YVRE, a benchmark suite focusing on content and AI safety evaluations
Yuvion VL-32B outperforms comparably sized open-source and commercial models in safety performance
summary: The paper introduces Yuvion VL, a multimodal foundation model designed to address adversarial risks in content and AI safety. It features specialized training pipelines including instruction-tuned and reasoning-oriented variants, an automated data synthesis pipeline with multi-stage quality control, and the Confuse-then-Contrast Fine-Tuning framework for enhanced discrimination of fine-grained visual-semantic elements. The model is rigorously evaluated using YVRE benchmarks, demonstrating superior safety performance compared to other models.
novelty: Introduces a new multimodal model with specialized training pipelines for adversarial robustness
audience: AI researchers and developers focused on security and adversarial machine learning

title: Erased, but Not Gone: Output Forgetting Is Not True Forgetting
why: This research challenges the current understanding of machine unlearning effectiveness by revealing that methods often appear to have forgotten data at the output level while retaining structured residuals in the model's internal representations.
notes: Machine unlearning (MU) is typically evaluated based on output forgetting, such as low forget-set accuracy or reduced logit-level membership inference
Current evaluations can systematically overestimate the success of unlearning when assessed through retraining-consistent representation forgetting
Retraining reveals a structured mismatch characterized by forget/retain asymmetry and directional residuals along retraining-related directions
The study uses multiple datasets, models, and unlearning methods to provide empirical evidence
summary: This paper investigates the effectiveness of machine unlearning (MU) through a lens that considers retraining-consistent representation forgetting. It finds that while current evaluations often show apparent output-level success in MU, they can overestimate actual forgetting when assessed against retrained models. The study reveals residual discrepancies concentrated along retraining-related directions rather than being diffuse across the model's internal representations. This suggests that current methods may appear to have forgotten data at the output level but retain structured residuals internally.
novelty: challenges current understanding of machine unlearning effectiveness through a stronger lens of retraining-consistent forgetting
audience: researchers and engineers working on machine learning model privacy and data protection

title: Internal Data Repetition Destroys Language Models
why: Understanding how repetition in training data affects large language models is crucial for optimizing their performance and resource allocation.
notes: Language models face a shortage of high-quality training data, leading to some degree of data repetition even after deduplication
Eval loss peaks at an intermediate repeat count, varying with model size according to a power law
When repeated documents consume 10% of FLOPs budget, the compute-equivalent loss can be significant (e.g., 67% of FLOPs for Qwen3)
A misspecified linear regression model reproduces the same qualitative loss peak, indicating statistical tradeoffs between memorization and generalization
Findings are not specific to language models but apply broadly in machine learning
summary: This study revisits the impact of data repetition on modern large language models using a fitted no-repetition scaling law. It finds that eval loss peaks at an intermediate repeat count, with the most damaging number of repeated data growing faster than compute allocation. When repeated documents consume 10% of FLOPs budget, significant compute-equivalent losses occur. The study also demonstrates that these phenomena can be explained by a misspecified linear regression model, highlighting statistical tradeoffs between memorization and generalization.
novelty: Analyzes repetition damage in modern large language models using a fitted no-repetition scaling law
audience: AI researchers and practitioners interested in language model training data quality

title: Perfect Detection, Failed Control: The Geometry of Knowing vs. Steering in Language Models
why: This research reveals the fundamental limitations of current language models' controllability despite their ability to detect certain behaviors with high accuracy.
notes: Detection of fake entities is perfectly linearly separable (AUC = 1.000 from layer 5)
The angle between detection and control directions for hallucination ranges from cos = 0.12 to cos = 0.20 across different model scales
Cosine similarity does not predict steerability, indicating a dissociation between knowing and steering in models
Gap persists before and after instruction tuning (cos = 0.1197 vs 0.1200)
A 15-degree rotation toward the refusal direction partially bridges the gap but introduces false positives
summary: The paper explores the geometric relationship between detection and control in language models, focusing on the behavior of detecting fake entities versus refusing to generate them. It finds that while models can detect fake entities with perfect accuracy (AUC = 1.000), the angle between the direction for detection and the direction for refusal is small but significant (cosine similarity ranging from 0.12 to 0.20). This gap persists across different model scales and after instruction tuning, indicating that detection does not imply control. The study also shows that cosine similarity alone cannot predict steerability, suggesting a complex functional relationship between knowing and steering behaviors.
novelty: Demonstrates the geometric gap between detection and control of behaviors in language models
audience: Mechanistic interpretability researchers and AI ethics practitioners

title: RAS: Measuring LLM Safety Through Refusal Alignment
why: RAS offers a new method for evaluating the safety of large language models by measuring internal representations rather than just output responses.
notes: Proposes SafeVec, an evaluation procedure that measures LLM safety from internal representations
Extracts layer-wise refusal directions from a safety-aligned reference model to score target models
RAS metric maps representation-level alignment to a calibrated 0-100 safety score
Across Llama, Gemma, and Qwen models, RAS separates aligned variants from uncensored/abliterated ones
Faster than judge-based evaluation methods
summary: The paper introduces SafeVec, a white-box evaluation procedure for measuring the safety of large language models (LLMs) through internal representations. It extracts layer-wise refusal directions from a reference model and scores target models based on their hidden state alignment with these directions under unsafe prompts. The resulting RAS metric provides a calibrated 0-100 safety score, demonstrating effectiveness across different LLM families like Llama, Gemma, and Qwen.
novelty: Introduces SafeVec for measuring internal representation-based safety in LLMs
audience: LLM researchers and security professionals

title: Security and Privacy in Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Architectures, Threats, Defenses, and Future Directions for Building Trustworthy Systems
why: This survey provides a comprehensive examination of security and privacy challenges in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, which are increasingly used to enhance large language models.
notes: Sensitive information may be exposed through retrieval indices, query logs, context construction, or federated updates
Adversarial manipulation of knowledge bases can undermine trust in generated outputs
Survey covers privacy and security challenges across centralized, on-device (Micro-RAG), federated, and hybrid RAG paradigms
Unified taxonomy of threat surfaces spans retrieval, context construction, and generation stages
Includes review of architectural, algorithmic, and cryptographic defenses with privacy-utility trade-offs
summary: This paper surveys the security and privacy challenges in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, which integrate retrieval mechanisms with generative models to enhance factual grounding and adaptability. It identifies new risks such as exposure of sensitive information through various components and adversarial manipulation of knowledge bases. The survey provides a taxonomy of threat surfaces across different deployment paradigms and analyzes attack classes including membership inference, index inference, poisoning, gradient leakage, and collusion. Additionally, it reviews defenses and discusses privacy-utility trade-offs for building trustworthy RAG systems.
novelty: Comprehensive examination of security and privacy challenges in Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems
audience: Security researchers and AI practitioners interested in trustworthy RAG systems

title: Detect, Unlearn, Restore: Defending Text Summarization Models Against Data Poisoning
why: This research introduces robust defenses against data poisoning in text summarization models, which is crucial for maintaining the integrity and reliability of AI systems.
notes: Training-time data poisoning can induce persistent failures like biased or harmful summaries without affecting standard evaluation metrics
Detection methods include influence-function analysis with semantic consistency checks (white-box) and behavioral auditing via semantics-preserving perturbations (black-box)
Defenses achieve 85-92% detection precision across nine architectures and six benchmark datasets under adaptive attacks
Gradient-ascent unlearning can restore up to 96% of original model behavior with minimal utility loss (<0.6% ROUGE degradation)
summary: The paper presents a unified post-hoc defense framework for detecting and remediating data poisoning in text summarization models during fine-tuning. It introduces novel attacks targeting factual distortion and representational bias, demonstrating that poisoned models exhibit abnormal training influence and sensitivity to perturbations. The study shows high detection precision (85-92%) across various architectures and datasets under adaptive attacks, with effective unlearning methods restoring model behavior without significant utility loss.
novelty: introduces novel attacks targeting factual distortion and representational bias in text summarization models
audience: researchers and engineers focused on AI security and large language model robustness

title: SoK: AI Secure Code Generation: Progress, Pitfalls, and Paths Forward
why: This study provides critical insights into the current capabilities and limitations of AI in generating secure code, which is essential for advancing cybersecurity practices.
notes: Introduces a three-level framework measuring models' understanding of secure coding principles versus their ability to actuate these principles during code generation
Explores techniques such as prompting, fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and agentic workflows for secure code generation
Results indicate that principle understanding is a strong predictor of functional correctness, security, and joint functional-security correctness
Identifies significant knowledge-actuation gaps where models fail to translate principles into secure and functional code
summary: The paper presents a systematic analysis of AI's role in generating secure code, introducing a three-level framework that evaluates models' understanding of secure coding principles and their ability to implement these principles during code generation. The study explores various techniques like prompting, fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and agentic workflows but finds substantial gaps between theoretical knowledge and practical application. Despite strong correlations between principle understanding and functional correctness, security, and joint functional-security correctness, models often fail to produce secure and functional code.
novelty: Three-level framework measuring natural-language understanding vs. actual actuation in secure coding
audience: AI researchers and developers focused on security

title: Constraint Tax in Open-Weight LLMs: An Empirical Study of Tool Calling Suppression Under Structured Output Constraints
why: Understanding and mitigating 'Tool Suppression' in open-weight LLMs is crucial for ensuring reliable agent systems that can effectively use tools under structured output constraints.
notes: Open-weight models cease invoking tools when Tool Calling and JSON Schema constraints are simultaneously enabled
JSON Schema constraints compile into grammar-based token masks, making tool-call tokens unreachable during decoding
Constraint Priority Inversion (CPI) hypothesis suggests schema satisfaction dominates action selection under multiple simultaneous constraints
Transparent Two-Pass Execution strategy restores tool invocation while preserving structured output guarantees without retraining models
summary: This study identifies a phenomenon called 'Tool Suppression' in open-weight large language models (LLMs), where models stop invoking tools when both Tool Calling and JSON Schema constraints are enabled. The research reveals that JSON Schema constraints compile into grammar-based token masks, making tool-call tokens unreachable during decoding. To address this issue, the authors propose the Constraint Priority Inversion hypothesis and introduce Transparent Two-Pass Execution as a mitigation strategy. Experimental results show that this approach restores tool invocation while maintaining structured output guarantees without requiring model retraining.
novelty: identifies and explains a new phenomenon of 'Tool Suppression' in open-weight LLMs
audience: researchers studying AI agents and model behavior under constraints

title: What Intermediate Layers Know: Detecting Jailbreaks from Entropy Dynamics
why: This research reveals how jailbreak attacks are encoded in intermediate layers of LLMs, offering a new approach to detecting harmful intent through entropy dynamics.
notes: Analyzes token-level predictive entropy trajectories across layers of frozen LLMs
Features capturing entropy evolution across token positions are more informative than static aggregate statistics
Signal is concentrated in intermediate layers and degrades at the final layer
Entropy dynamics provide consistent separation without additional training
Study involves multiple models (Llama, Qwen, Gemma) and adversarial benchmarks
summary: The study investigates how jailbreak attacks are encoded within large language models' internal representations by analyzing token-level predictive entropy trajectories across layers. It finds that features capturing the evolution of entropy across token positions in intermediate layers are more informative for detecting harmful intent than static aggregate statistics. The research demonstrates consistent separation without additional training, indicating that jailbreak-relevant structure is most pronounced in mid-network representations.
novelty: Analyzing entropy evolution across token positions to detect jailbreaks in LLMs
audience: Security researchers and AI model developers

title: LLM Evolution as an Industry-Scale Ecosystem: A Lifecycle Perspective on Continual Learning
why: This paper provides a critical framework for understanding and implementing industrial-scale continual learning in large language models (LLMs), which is essential for their continuous improvement without retraining from scratch.
notes: Continual learning capability is crucial for Industrial LLMs to adapt to evolving requirements
ICL reformulated as a closed-loop update-and-release problem within a versioned ecosystem
Three core challenges: repeated adaptation erodes model plasticity, foundation-model upgrades break inheritance, long-term sustainability constrained by deployment needs

Five lifecycle design principles outlined for ICL: preserving plasticity headroom, treating upgrades as capability transfer, enabling trustworthy continual reinforcement learning, self-optimizing training recipes, building accountability base layer
summary: The paper discusses the importance of continual learning in large language models (LLMs) to meet industrial needs without retraining from scratch. It reformulates Industrial Continual Learning (ICL) as a closed-loop update-and-release process within a versioned ecosystem where updates propagate hierarchically. The study identifies three core challenges: repeated adaptation erodes model plasticity, foundation-model upgrades break capability inheritance, and long-term sustainability is constrained by deployment requirements. To address these issues, the paper outlines five lifecycle design principles for ICL: preserving plasticity headroom, treating upgrades as capability transfer, enabling trustworthy continual reinforcement learning, making training recipes self-optimizing, and building accountability as a base layer for long-term iteration.
novelty: Reformulates Industrial Continual Learning (ICL) for LLMs as a closed-loop update-and-release problem
audience: Senior software engineers and ML researchers interested in continual learning and large language model evolution

title: Dense Supervision Is Not Enough: The Readout Blind Spot in Looped Language Models
why: This research highlights a critical flaw in how recurrent scale control is managed in looped language models, which could impact model performance and stability.
notes: Looped language models transform hidden states into runtime state for prediction
Dense per-loop cross-entropy only controls variables exposed by the readout, not all active in transitions
Scale-invariant readouts like RMSNorm hide radial scale from immediate loss
Pre-norm residual recurrence carries and updates hidden-state scale without control
Inter-loop normalization is necessary to prevent uncontrolled growth of norms
summary: The paper identifies a 'readout blind spot' in looped language models, where dense supervision through cross-entropy does not effectively manage the scale of recurrent states. Scale-invariant readouts like RMSNorm and LayerNorm hide radial scale from immediate loss, while pre-norm residual recurrence continues to update hidden-state scale unchecked. Without inter-loop normalization, this can lead to uncontrolled growth in norms, impacting model performance. The study suggests that making scale visible to the loss or removing it from the loop are necessary design principles for recurrent scale control.
novelty: Identifies a critical blind spot in how dense supervision affects recurrent scale control in looped language models.
audience: Researchers and engineers working on advanced natural language processing systems.

title: Small edits, large models: How Wikipedia advocacy shapes LLM values
why: This study shows how targeted Wikipedia editing can significantly influence language model behavior in specific topic areas, highlighting the power of small groups to shape AI values.
notes: PAW (Pro-Animal Wikipedians) made 125 edits across 115 pages
Gradient-based data attribution showed PAW-edited sections accounted for 68% of highest-attributed documents for animal welfare queries in Llama 3.1 8B model
MAGIC counterfactual influence estimation found top-10 most influential documents on animal welfare queries were all PAW edits across five random training-order seeds
Fine-tuning separate models on PAW content versus control content improved perplexity scores specifically for the type of text each was trained on
summary: The study 'Small edits, large models: How Wikipedia advocacy shapes LLM values' demonstrates that a small group of volunteers can significantly influence language model behavior in specific topic areas through targeted Wikipedia editing. Using gradient-based data attribution and MAGIC counterfactual influence estimation, the research shows that PAW (Pro-Animal Wikipedians) edits are highly influential for animal welfare queries in large language models like Llama 3.1 8B. Fine-tuning separate models on PAW content versus control content also improved perplexity scores specifically for the type of text each was trained on, further confirming the impact of targeted editing.
novelty: Demonstrates how targeted Wikipedia editing can significantly influence language model behavior in specific topic areas.
audience: AI researchers and practitioners interested in understanding the impact of training data on model outputs.

title: The Unfireable Safety Kernel: Execution-Time AI Alignment for AI Agents and Other Escapable AI Systems
why: The Unfireable Safety Kernel offers a novel execution-time safety mechanism for AI systems that could prevent unauthorized access or malicious actions by ensuring fail-closed behavior.
notes: The Unfireable Safety Kernel is implemented in Rust and satisfies four key properties: process separation, pre-action enforcement on a structurally only path, fail-closed at both the request and system levels, and externalized signed evidence verifiable outside the controlled system's trust boundary
Machine-checked invariants include an SMT theorem (Z3) and an exhaustive bounded-model-checking proof of the production decision function (Kani)
In live tests against an escape-seeking adversary, all 704 attempts on the safety-critical core were refused with no escape across 1,000 self-modifications
The system also resisted 300 attempts under the operator kill switch and 6,240 authorization round-trips without successful bypass
summary: This paper introduces the Unfireable Safety Kernel, a Rust reference implementation designed to ensure architectural control over AI systems by enforcing execution-time safety mechanisms. The kernel satisfies four critical properties: process separation, pre-action enforcement on a structurally only path, fail-closed behavior at both request and system levels, and externalized signed evidence verifiable outside the controlled system's trust boundary. It includes machine-checked invariants with an SMT theorem (Z3) and bounded-model-checking proof (Kani). In live tests against adversarial attacks, the kernel successfully prevented all unauthorized access attempts.
novelty: Introduces a new safety mechanism for AI systems that operates at execution time rather than training or inference time
audience: AI researchers and engineers focused on security and alignment of autonomous systems

title: Cliff Tokens: Identifying Single-Token Failure Triggers in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
why: Identifies the exact token-level triggers that cause large language models (LLMs) to fail in mathematical reasoning tasks, offering a new method for improving model reliability and performance.
notes: Introduces 'cliff tokens' where the potential for correct reasoning drops significantly
Across seven LLMs and three benchmarks: GSM1K, MATH500, AIME 2025
Deleting the first cliff token recovers pass@64 to 1.0; keeping it limits recovery between 0.71-1.00
Taxonomy includes deterministic, uncertain, and sampled-off cliffs based on greedy choice and token entropy
Cliff-DPO trained on GSM8K improves accuracy by up to +6.6 across benchmarks
summary: The paper introduces 'cliff tokens' as the precise single-token triggers that cause LLMs to fail in mathematical reasoning tasks. By identifying these tokens, researchers can improve model performance: deleting a cliff token and resampling leads to full recovery of pass@64 accuracy (1.0), while retaining it limits recovery between 0.71-1.00. The study also presents a taxonomy for classifying cliffs into deterministic, uncertain, and sampled-off types based on greedy choice and entropy measures. Training with Cliff-DPO on GSM8K improves model accuracy by up to +6.6 across various benchmarks.
novelty: Identifies precise token-level triggers for failure in LLM mathematical reasoning
audience: LLM researchers and developers interested in improving model reliability

title: To Isolate or to Score? Model-Adaptive Assessment for Cost-Efficient Multi-Agent RAG
why: The study introduces MADARA, an efficient model-adaptive routing architecture that significantly reduces the computational cost of multi-agent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems.
notes: Study focuses on 7B-9B instruction-tuned models across diverse QA benchmarks
Weaker baseline models benefit from per-document isolation rather than full assessment, showing up to a 50 percentage point improvement
Stronger baselines require scoring quality for performance enhancement
Reasoning-Score Coupling is introduced as a label-free perturbation probe to classify scoring behavior
MADARA's diagnostic thresholds generalize zero-shot across four unseen model families
summary: The paper explores cost-efficient assessment mechanisms in multi-agent RAG systems, revealing that weaker baseline models benefit more from per-document isolation than full multi-agent assessment. For stronger baselines, the study introduces Reasoning-Score Coupling to enhance scoring quality without labels. The findings lead to MADARA, a model-adaptive routing architecture that eliminates computational overhead and generalizes across different model families.
novelty: Introduces Reasoning-Score Coupling and MADARA, addressing cost-efficiency in multi-agent RAG
audience: Researchers and practitioners working on retrieval-augmented generation systems

title: Do vision-language models search like humans? Reasoning tokens as a reaction-time analog in classic visual-search paradigms
why: This study provides a novel method for assessing machine visual cognition using reasoning tokens, offering insights into how vision-language models process visual information and compare with human cognitive patterns.
notes: Uses reasoning tokens as an analog to reaction time in visual search tasks
Compares four classic paradigms: feature vs conjunction search, spatial configuration (T-vs-L), enumeration, tilted/vertical asymmetry
Frontier models maintain accuracy while mid-tier collapse under certain conditions
Models show target-present effort slope exceeds target-absent slope, diverging from human behavior
Enumeration remains accurate in models where humans would lose count
summary: The study explores whether vision-language models exhibit the same behavioral signatures as humans in visual search tasks by using reasoning tokens to measure cognitive effort. It adapts four classic paradigms and finds that while some patterns align with human behavior, others diverge significantly, indicating unique computational strategies within these models.
novelty: uses reasoning tokens as a reaction-time analog for machine visual cognition studies
audience: researchers studying vision-language models and cognitive science

title: Diagnosing and Mitigating Compounding Failures in Agentic Persuasion via Taxonomic Strategy Retrieval
why: This research introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), a novel approach that mitigates compounding errors in agentic persuasion, which is crucial for improving the reliability and effectiveness of AI agents in subjective tasks.
notes: Identifies semantic leakage in standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) as a trigger for compounding errors
Introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG) to decouple argumentative structure from topical content
Zero-shot, cross-domain evaluations show TS-RAG improves the transfer of abstract logic
Empowers lightweight persuaders to defeat parametrically superior opponents, improving win rates from 70.5% to 78.5%
Introduces trace-level diagnostics via Debate State Representation (DSR)
summary: The paper addresses compounding errors in agentic persuasion by identifying semantic leakage as a key issue in standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). It introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), which decouples argumentative structure from topical content, significantly improving the transfer of abstract logic across domains. TS-RAG enables lightweight agents to outperform more powerful opponents and enhances argumentative efficiency through trace-level diagnostics via Debate State Representation (DSR).
novelty: introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG) as a solution to semantic leakage in standard RAG
audience: researchers and engineers working on AI agents and persuasion systems

title: Natural Ungrokking: Asymmetric Control of Which Rules Survive Pretraining
why: This research reveals how language models can spontaneously forget learned linguistic rules during training, challenging our understanding of model stability and generalization.
notes: A small language model learns the pronoun-gender rule with an accuracy of 0.94 by step 925 but scores near zero by step 3,500 despite evidence remaining in the training data
Rule survival is predictable based on support frequency from corpus statistics across different runs and budgets
Forgetting occurs through displacement where competing patterns out-compete learned rules within 100 steps
Control over rule survival is asymmetric: destroying a rule cannot be reversed by injecting support, even at high levels
summary: The paper introduces 'natural ungrokking,' the phenomenon of language models spontaneously forgetting learned linguistic rules during pretraining. By step 925, a model accurately resolves pronouns based on gender cues but loses this ability by step 3,500 despite continued exposure to relevant training data. The survival of such rules is determined by their frequency in the corpus and not influenced by loss curve changes. Forgetting happens through displacement where competing patterns out-compete learned rules within a short period (100 steps). Additionally, once a rule is forgotten, it cannot be restored even with increased support.
novelty: Within-run reversal of learned linguistic rules without trace in loss curve
audience: ML researchers and linguists studying language model behavior

title: Swarm-Inspired Generation of Collective Behaviors in Graph Dynamical Systems
why: SIES offers a novel approach to learning generalizable coupling operators for synchronization control and adaptive robot coordination, which could revolutionize the design of complex systems in robotics and beyond.
notes: SIES framework learns local-interaction laws that produce desired global organization across various network scales without retraining
Uses signed source-target-conditioned attention as an adaptive coupling term within an explicit evolution model
Outperforms three oscillator baselines in reaching gait-related modes faster and generalizes synchronization-driven locomotion to multi-legged robots of different scales
Achieves highest performance on heterophilous node-classification benchmarks compared to other methods
summary: The Swarm-Inspired Emergent Synchronizer (SIES) framework is introduced for learning generalizable local-interaction laws in graph dynamical systems. Each node acts as an agent-like unit with a state and task cue, employing signed source-target-conditioned attention as an adaptive coupling term within an explicit evolution model. This approach enables synchronization control across untrained network scales without retraining, outperforming three oscillator baselines in reaching gait-related modes faster. Additionally, SIES demonstrates superior performance on heterophilous node-classification benchmarks compared to other methods.
novelty: Introduces SIES framework for learning generalizable coupling operators in graph dynamical systems
audience: AI researchers and developers interested in collective behavior, synchronization control, and adaptive robot coordination

title: Conformal Orbit-Valid Trust Horizons for Equivariant World Models
why: This research introduces a method for certifying trust horizons in equivariant world models, which could enhance the reliability of AI systems by ensuring their predictions remain accurate over longer time periods.
notes: The conformal factor γ_α is 1.0 under audit protocol, indicating that raw certificates are already conservative
Across 50 stable audits, zero anti-conservative violations were observed
Exact equivariance transports a calibrated trust-horizon curve over the group orbit when certain conditions are met
Empirical results show small orbit-transport residuals with median 1.1% and maximum 4.1%
The certificate is a conservative distributional audit, not a global reachability guarantee
summary: This paper explores trust-horizon certification for latent world models that have known group symmetries. It introduces a method to form raw horizon curves using one-step latent residuals and finite-time expansion estimates, then calibrates them with split-conformal multiplicative factors. The study finds no anti-conservative violations across 50 stable audits, indicating the raw certificate is already conservative under audit protocol. Key findings include exact equivariance transporting a calibrated trust-horizon curve over the group orbit when specific conditions are satisfied and empirical results showing small orbit-transport residuals. Additionally, the paper highlights two complementary regimes for model validation: symmetric substrates where all models exhibit similar robustness, and more complex 3D environments where equivariant models outperform non-equivariant ones.
novelty: Introduces a method for certifying trust horizons in equivariant world models
audience: Researchers interested in machine learning theory and model certification

title: Neural operator-based digital twins for modeling amyloid-$\beta$ and tau propagation and treatment optimization in Alzheimer's disease
why: This research advances the use of neural operators in creating digital twins that can predict the progression of Alzheimer's disease with high accuracy, enabling more precise therapeutic interventions.
notes: Predictive accuracy: 87% for amyloid-$\beta$, 81% for tau proteins
Uses reaction-diffusion dynamics to model biomarker propagation on cortical surface
Incorporates operator learning and reduced-order representations to infer governing equations from sparse, noisy data
Formulates a PDE-constrained optimal control problem for personalized treatment design
summary: The article presents a novel framework using neural operators to create patient-specific digital twins that model the propagation of amyloid-$\beta$ and tau proteins in Alzheimer's disease. By leveraging reaction-diffusion dynamics, operator learning, and reduced-order representations, the method achieves high predictive accuracy from sparse clinical data. The approach also formulates an optimal control problem for designing personalized therapeutic strategies to regulate pathological protein propagation.
novelty: Neural operator-based digital twins infer governing equations of disease progression from sparse, noisy clinical data.
audience: Researchers in AI-driven medical modeling and neurodegenerative disease treatment.

title: The Unbearable Cheapness of Open Weight Models
why: The economic implications of open vs closed AI models could shift the competitive landscape, potentially making open-weight models like DeepSeek V4 more accessible and cost-effective than 'frontier' models.
notes: DeepSeek V4 is nearly a 50x cheaper than Anthropic and OpenAI's frontier models based on token pricing
Google released Gemma 4 in April 2026, while Meta’s LLaMA hasn’t had an update since then
OpenAI last released open weight GPT models in 2025; Anthropic has never released any open weight model
Allen AI is developing a fully open AI with support from NSF and Nvidia partnership
summary: The article discusses the economic implications of open vs closed AI models, focusing on DeepSeek V4's significantly lower pricing compared to frontier models like those from Anthropic and OpenAI. It questions whether these companies can reduce their prices to compete or if they will rely on scarcity tactics to maintain high costs. The piece also highlights recent developments in true open-source models, such as Allen AI’s partnership with NSF and Nvidia for a fully open AI system.
novelty: Discussion on the economic implications of open vs closed AI models
audience: AI researchers and policymakers concerned about market dynamics in AI

title: From Meta Idea to Advanced Mathematical Discovery -- Human-AI Co-Discovery of Sign-Embedding Quantum Algorithms
why: This study demonstrates the potential for AI to significantly enhance human intuition in formulating complex mathematical problems and discovering new quantum algorithms.
notes: Human-AI co-discovery led to sign-embedding quantum algorithms for matrix equations and functions
Rational approximation of jump-type functions, like the sign function, was used as a design principle
AI-assisted exploration expanded initial intuition into a concrete problem-solving route map
AIM system connected known identities to wider classes of problems and drafted proofs
Human decision-making remained crucial for validating AI-generated hypotheses
summary: This paper explores the human-AI co-discovery process that led to sign-embedding quantum algorithms, foundational in quantum linear algebra. The research began with a human intuition about rational approximation's effectiveness for jump-type functions like the sign function. AI-assisted exploration expanded this intuition into concrete problem-solving routes and compared candidate formulations. The AIM system played a key role by connecting known matrix-sign identities to broader classes of problems and drafting proofs, while humans made critical decisions such as rejecting certain approximations due to hidden conditions.
novelty: AI-assisted exploration in problem formation and connection discovery for quantum algorithms
audience: Researchers interested in AI-assisted mathematics and quantum computing

title: ASAP: Agent-System Co-Design for Wall-Clock-Centered Auto HPO Research for ML Experiments
why: ASAP addresses the limitations of current HPO tools by integrating diverse optimizers and reducing wall-clock time, potentially improving the efficiency and effectiveness of machine learning experiments.
notes: ASAP uses an LLM to integrate a pool of inductive-biased optimizers
LLM inference and tool execution are paid serially on top of model evaluation every round
A prefix-stable prompt maximizes KV-cache reuse across rounds
Speculation parallelism hides remaining latency under model evaluation via relative-error accept test
ASAP outperforms baselines in extensive experiments on diverse HPO tasks
summary: The ASAP framework addresses the limitations of current hyperparameter optimization (HPO) tools by integrating a diverse pool of inductive-biased optimizers using an LLM. It re-architects the loop to reduce end-to-end wall-clock time while preserving regret quality through techniques like KV-cache reuse and speculation parallelism, which hide latency under model evaluation. Extensive experiments show that ASAP outperforms existing baselines on diverse HPO tasks.
novelty: Agent-system co-design for HPO that integrates diverse optimizers and reduces wall-clock time
audience: ML researchers focusing on hyperparameter optimization

title: Training for the Model You Return: Improving Optimization for Iterate-Averaged Language Models
why: The paper introduces PACE, an optimizer that improves the performance of iterate-averaged language models, which is a common practice in modern machine learning pipelines.
notes: Formulates optimizer design for iterate-average estimator as an optimal-control problem
Develops PACE, a lightweight wrapper around AdamW with clipped per-coordinate control strength
Proves stylized version of PACE converges at standard stochastic convex optimization rate up to a factor depending on the averaging rule
Empirically shows improvement in supervised fine-tuning and GPT-2 pretraining for various hyperparameters
summary: The paper addresses the challenge of optimizing iterate-averaged language models by formulating optimizer design as an optimal-control problem. It introduces PACE, a lightweight wrapper around AdamW that pulls live weights toward their exponential moving average with clipped per-coordinate control strength. Theoretical analysis shows PACE can improve convergence rates and empirical results demonstrate its effectiveness in supervised fine-tuning of large LMs and GPT-2 pretraining.
novelty: Formulates optimizer design for iterate-average estimator as an optimal-control problem
audience: ML researchers focused on language model optimization

title: LLM-ACES: Closed-Loop Discovery of Dynamical Systems with LLM-Guided Adaptive Search
